30: A direct revelation: 'But God raised him from the dead; and for many days he appeared to those who had come up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are now his witnesses to the people.' Paul at Pisidian Antioch places the resurrection as the pivotal event that all of Israel's history was pointing toward — the promises to the patriarchs (Acts 13:32), the Davidic covenant (Acts 13:34), and the resurrection prophecies of the Psalms (Psalms 2:7; Psalms 16:10; Isaiah 55:3) all converge on this fact. The witnesses from Galilee are the guarantors of historical continuity: the risen one is the same Jesus who walked those roads. Christology is not mythology but testified history.
47: A fulfillment: Paul cites Isaiah 49:6 ('I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth') as the scriptural mandate for the Gentile mission. The Servant's commission is now Christ's commission extended through Paul and Barnabas. This is typological fulfillment: the Servant who could not be restricted to Israel's restoration alone (Isaiah 49:6a) prefigures Christ whose mission overflows every ethnic and geographic boundary. Paul's application of the Servant commission to himself and Barnabas (first person plural) shows that missionaries participate in Christ's Servant identity — they are his instruments in the fulfillment of Isa 49.
1: The Antioch church's prophets and teachers are listed before the Spirit's commissioning of Barnabas and Saul. The Christological significance: the first intentional missionary sending originates not in Jerusalem but in this multiethnic, Hellenistic community — the Spirit is not bound to the established center but moves where the Christological community worships and listens. The list itself (Barnabas, Simeon Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen, Saul) is the most ethnically diverse leadership body in Acts.
2: 'While they were worshipping the Lord (leitourgountōn de autōn tō kyriō) and fasting, the Holy Spirit said: 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.' The Spirit's voice during worship and fasting is the Christological commission for the Gentile mission. The missionary call does not arise from strategic planning but from attentive listening during worship directed to the Lord Jesus. Christ commissions through his Spirit what he commanded in his ascension: witness to the ends of the earth.
3: The laying on of hands and sending away — the Antioch church's liturgical act of commissioning — is the community's participation in what the Spirit has initiated. The Christological pattern: the Spirit calls, the community confirms. Neither acts independently. The sending is simultaneously the Spirit's (ekpemphthenotes hypo tou hagiou pneumatos, Acts 13:4 — 'sent out by the Holy Spirit') and the church's. Missionary authority flows through both divine commission and community confirmation.
4: 'So, being sent out by the Holy Spirit, they went down to Seleucia, and from there they sailed to Cyprus' — the Spirit's sending connects directly to Christ's commission (Acts 1:8 — 'you will be my witnesses'). Cyprus is Barnabas's home territory (Acts 4:36); the Spirit's routing begins where the missionary has natural connections. The Christological wisdom: the Spirit works through the apostles' existing relationships and backgrounds, not despite them. The gospel uses the human network the Spirit shaped before the commission was given.
5: Salamis synagogue proclamation — 'they proclaimed the word of God in the synagogues of the Jews' — establishes the first-journey pattern: beginning with the synagogue audience that has the scriptural foundation for receiving the Christological argument. John Mark as assistant (hypēretēs) parallels Jesus's own pattern of sending disciples out in pairs (Luke 10:1). The Christological mission strategy: start with those who have the prophetic preparation, then extend to those without it.
6: The encounter with Bar-Jesus ('son of Jesus/Joshua') — a Jewish false prophet and magician — at the court of Sergius Paulus places Paul before a high-ranking Roman official with a counterfeit guide. The Christological irony of the name: 'Bar-Jesus' (son of the Savior) is the designation of a man who actively opposes the true Jesus. The spiritual battle is for the soul of the proconsul; the false prophet is the agent of the 'god of this world who has blinded the minds of unbelievers' (2 Corinthians 4:4).
7: Sergius Paulus 'summoned Barnabas and Saul and sought to hear the word of God' (epezētēsen akousai ton logon tou theou) — the Roman proconsul's genuine intellectual and spiritual openness is the occasion for the encounter. The Christological mission reaches into the highest levels of Roman administration. Christ's promise that his witnesses would stand before 'governors and kings' (Matthew 10:18; Acts 9:15) is being fulfilled systematically as the mission advances from synagogues to palaces.
8: Elymas the magician 'withstood them, seeking to turn the proconsul away from the faith' — the Christological conflict is explicit: this is not merely a personal dispute but a contest over whether Sergius Paulus will move toward or away from faith in the risen Christ. Every conversion is a transfer of sovereignty: from the domain of the false to the domain of the true Lord. The opponent of conversion is simultaneously an opponent of Christ's lordship over his own created image-bearer.
9: 'But Saul, who was also called Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, looked intently at him' — the first use of the name Paul in Acts marks the Roman mission's beginning. The Spirit-filling for this specific confrontation (the Spirit acts situationally, not merely permanently) grants discernment and authority. The 'intent gaze' (atenisas) is the same focused attention Peter used before healing the lame man (Acts 3:4) — the prelude to a decisive act in Christ's name.
10: 'You son of the devil, you enemy of all righteousness, full of all deceit and villainy, will you not stop making crooked the straight paths of the Lord?' — Paul's denunciation identifies Elymas with the adversarial power that opposes Christ's mission. 'Making crooked the straight paths of the Lord' echoes Isaiah 40:3-4 (the highway-preparation prophecy applied to John the Baptist, Luke 3:4-5) — Elymas is doing the opposite of John's preparatory work. Where John straightened the way for Christ, Elymas curves it. The Christological mission has its counterfeit counter-mission.
11: The blindness judgment on Elymas — 'immediately mist and darkness fell on him' — is a temporary blindness mirroring Paul's own Damascus road blindness (Acts 9:8-9). The Christological irony: Paul was blinded on the road to Damascus when Christ revealed himself; Elymas is blinded when he tries to prevent Christ's revelation reaching another. The same risen Christ who struck Paul blind to open his eyes (Acts 9:17-18) strikes Elymas blind through Paul. The judgment is Christologically administered.
12: Sergius Paulus believed, 'astonished at the teaching of the Lord' (ekplēssomenos epi tē didachē tou kyriou) — the Roman proconsul's conversion is described as astonishment at the Lord's teaching, not merely at the miracle of blinding. The Christological priority: the miracle prompts attention; the teaching produces faith. The sign is the servant of the word; the word about the risen Lord is what creates belief. The first Gentile conversion on the first missionary journey is a Roman official who is 'astonished at the teaching of the Lord.'
13: Paul and his companions sailed from Paphos to Perga in Pamphylia, where John Mark departed and returned to Jerusalem. The desertion of John Mark is the event that will cause the Barnabas-Paul split in Acts 15:36-41. The Christological community experiences internal conflict and failure as well as external opposition — neither the mission's divine commissioning nor its apostolic leadership prevents the ordinary human tensions that test every partnership.
14: Arriving at Pisidian Antioch and going into the synagogue on the Sabbath — the missionary pattern repeats: Sabbath, synagogue, opportunity to speak. The Christological witness seeks the prepared soil first. The synagogue audience has the Scriptures that Paul's sermon will open; the reading of the Law and the Prophets (Acts 13:15) provides the canonical framework within which the Christological proclamation makes sense. The message is new but not discontinuous — it fulfills what the audience already reveres.
15: The synagogue rulers' invitation — 'Brothers, if you have any word of encouragement (logos paraklēseōs) for the people, say it' — provides Paul his opening. The Christological word is simultaneously proclamation and consolation: the gospel of the risen Christ is the supreme word of encouragement (paraklēsis) that fulfills what the Law and the Prophets promised. The synagogue's liturgical hospitality becomes the occasion for the most developed Pauline sermon in Acts.
16: Paul's gesture — standing up and motioning with his hand — mirrors the classical orator's stance. His address to 'Men of Israel and you who fear God' identifies his dual audience: ethnic Jews and God-fearing Gentiles who had been attending synagogue. The Christological mission strategy targets both simultaneously: those who have the full covenant background and those who have come partway. Both groups are prepared differently but will receive the same Christological proclamation.
17: 'The God of this people Israel chose our fathers and made the people great during their stay in the land of Egypt, and with uplifted arm he led them out of it' — Paul's survey of Israel's history begins with Exodus because the Christ-event is the new and greater Exodus. The same pattern that structured Stephen's speech (Acts 7) structures Paul's: Israel's history is the preparation for Christ. The 'uplifted arm' that brought Israel out of Egypt is the same divine power that raised Jesus from the dead.
18: 'And for about forty years he put up with them in the wilderness' — the forty years of patient divine endurance during the wilderness period is the type of Christ's patience with a failing people. The same God who sustained Israel through its failure in the wilderness (Exodus 16:35; Numbers 14:33-34) is the God who sustained the community of Jesus through the repeated failures of the disciples. Divine patience is the Christological mode of God's relationship with the covenant community through history.
19: The destruction of the Canaanite nations and Israel's inheritance of the land (citing Joshua 14:1) — seven nations destroyed over approximately 450 years — establishes God's pattern: promise, delay, fulfillment. The Christological typology: the inheritance of the land under Joshua prefigures the inheritance of the nations under Jesus (same name: Iēsous/Yehoshua). What Joshua accomplished for Israel in Canaan, Jesus accomplishes for the new Israel in the world.
20: The period of judges through Samuel — the leadership sequence from conquest to monarchy — traces the covenant community's governance before the Davidic kingship. The Christological survey: God's patience through the cyclical failure of judges (Judges 2:11-19) is the pattern of divine faithfulness despite human unfaithfulness, culminating in the Davidic covenant whose ultimate fulfillment is Christ. Every judge is a provisional deliverer pointing toward the permanent king.
21: Saul the Benjaminite given as king, then rejected — the irony for the speaker (Saul of Tarsus, also a Benjaminite) is not subtle. The Christological reading of Saul's kingship: it was Israel's demand for 'a king like the nations' (1 Samuel 8:5), not God's initiative. The subsequent giving of David ('a man after my heart', Acts 13:22; 1 Samuel 13:14) contrasts the human-selected and divinely-selected king. Christ is the ultimate fulfillment of the divine-selection pattern: not Israel's demand but God's own appointment.
22: 'I have found in David the son of Jesse a man after my heart, who will do all my will' — the Davidic testimony (combining 1 Samuel 13:14 and Psalms 89:20) is Paul's bridge to the Christological announcement. David is the type because he is 'after God's heart' — the one whose will aligns with God's. Jesus is the antitype: the one who perfectly does 'not my will, but yours' (Luke 22:42 — the Gethsemane surrender). The one whose will perfectly aligned with the Father's is the one David's kingship anticipated.
23: 'From his offspring God has brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus, as he promised' — the programmatic Christological declaration arrives after the patriarchal-Davidic survey: the entire history was prologue to this. The Savior from David's offspring fulfills the Davidic covenant's deepest promise (2 Samuel 7:12-16; Psalms 89:3-4; Psalms 132:11). Paul's Christological announcement is not an innovation but the completion of what every Jewish worshipper had been waiting for through the entire canonical history he has just rehearsed.
24: John's preparatory baptism of repentance — 'before his coming' (pro prosōpou tēs eisodou autou, before the face of his entry) — establishes the Baptist as the forerunner whose ministry defines what preceded Christ. The Christological significance of John's place: he is the last prophet of the old order, the final voice before the one he announced. Paul's citation of John's testimony is both apologetic (Jewish audiences knew the Baptist) and theological (the forerunner validates the Coming One).
25: John's own self-testimony — 'What do you suppose that I am? I am not he. No, but behold, after me one is coming, the sandals of whose feet I am not worthy to untie' — is Paul's citation of the Baptist's Christological humility. John, the greatest of the prophets (Luke 7:28), claims himself unworthy of the most servile act toward Jesus. The Christological principle: every prophet and every servant exists to point beyond themselves to Christ. Authentic ministry is always self-diminishing and Christ-magnifying.
26: 'Brothers, sons of the family of Abraham, and those among you who fear God, to us has been sent the message of this salvation' — the Christological word of salvation addresses both Jew (sons of Abraham) and God-fearer (Gentile) simultaneously. The 'message of salvation' (ho logos tēs sōtērias tautēs) is a Lukan summary for the gospel of the risen Christ. The salvation announced is specific (this salvation — referring to everything the survey of history was building toward) and universal in address (to us — both covenant heirs and attached Gentiles).
27: 'For those who live in Jerusalem and their rulers, because they did not recognize him nor understand the utterances of the prophets that are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by condemning him' — the Christological irony of the crucifixion: those who heard the prophets every Sabbath but did not understand them fulfilled those very prophecies by their misunderstanding. The passion was simultaneously the greatest crime (deliberate miscarriage of justice) and the greatest fulfillment (every Sabbath's readings accomplished). God's purposes run through, not around, human blindness and malice.
28: 'Though they found in him no guilt worthy of death, they asked Pilate to have him executed' — the fourfold innocence declaration pattern (Pilate three times, Herod once in Luke's Gospel) that Luke has established is summarized here: Jesus was condemned without just cause. The Christological apologetic: the crucifixion was not the exposure of a criminal but the execution of an innocent. God's pattern of vindicating the righteous sufferer (Ps 22; 69; Isa 53) required first the unjust condemnation that the resurrection would reverse.
29: 'When they had carried out all that was written of him, they took him down from the tree and laid him in a tomb' — 'all that was written' (hapanta ta peri autou gegrammena) is Paul's comprehensive claim: the entire passion narrative fulfilled scriptural prophecy. The 'tree' (xylon) citation echoes Deuteronomy 21:23 ('cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree') — Paul will develop this explicitly in Galatians 3:13. The burial ('laid him in a tomb') establishes the death as real and verifiable: witnesses saw the body placed, which makes the subsequent resurrection appearance the more evidentially powerful.
31: 'He appeared for many days to those who had come up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are now his witnesses to the people' — the post-resurrection appearances to the Galilean companions are the evidentiary foundation of Paul's proclamation. The witnesses 'who are now his witnesses to the people' are the contemporary Jerusalem community (the Twelve and the 500+ of 1 Corinthians 15:6) who can still be interrogated. The Christological testimony is historically verifiable in principle: the witnesses are alive and the testimony is current.
32: 'And we bring you the good news that what God promised to the fathers, this he has fulfilled to us their children by raising Jesus' — the resurrection is the fulfillment-event that all the promises pointed toward. The Christological gospel is simultaneously the continuation of Israel's story (what God promised to the fathers) and the event that consummates it (raising Jesus). Paul is not announcing a departure from Israel's hope but its arrival. The promise-fulfillment structure makes the gospel the completion of the canonical narrative, not its replacement.
33: Psalms 2:7 applied to the resurrection — 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you' — is Paul's first OT proof text for the resurrection. The 'today' of the psalm is interpreted as the resurrection day: the day the Father publicly declared Jesus to be his Son by raising him from the dead (cf. Romans 1:4 — 'declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead'). The coronation psalm's royal declaration is the resurrection's theological content.
34: Isaiah 55:3 ('I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David') — the second OT proof. The 'holy and sure blessings' (ta hosia Dauid ta pista) are permanent covenant mercies that death cannot revoke. Paul's argument: these promises were made to David but could not be fulfilled in David (who died); they require a Davidic descendant who does not see corruption. The resurrection makes the Davidic blessings permanent and universal — available to 'everyone' (Acts 13:39) rather than restricted to ethnic Israel.
35: Psalms 16:10 ('you will not let your Holy One see corruption') — the third OT proof, the same psalm Peter used at Pentecost (Acts 2:27-31). Paul's application: David died, was buried, and saw corruption; therefore the psalm's promise is not about David but about the one David prophetically described. The resurrection is the historical event in which God kept the promise to his Holy One — the tomb was empty, the body was not subject to decomposition. The convergence of three independent OT witnesses to the same event is Paul's cumulative proof-by-Scripture.
36: 'For David, after he had served the purpose of God in his own generation, fell asleep and was laid with his fathers and saw corruption' — the finality of David's death is Paul's crucial distinction. David was 'the man after God's heart' who 'served the purpose of God in his own generation' — and yet he died and his body decayed. The Christological argument: if David fulfilled God's purpose but still died, the Psalms he wrote that speak of no-corruption must refer to someone else. The typological gap between David and his greater son requires the resurrection to fill it.
37: 'But he whom God raised up did not see corruption' — the resurrection is the fact that distinguishes Jesus from every previous righteous sufferer and fulfills every promise-without-precedent in the OT. No one before Jesus had been raised to immortal resurrection life; everyone else who was raised (Lazarus, Jairus's daughter) died again. Jesus's resurrection is categorically different: a once-for-all transformation into imperishable life. This is the event that validates the entire Christological reading of Israel's Scripture.
38: 'Let it be known to you therefore, brothers, that through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you' — the Christological implications of the resurrection-proclamation are soteriological: the one God raised is also the one through whom sins are forgiven. The resurrection vindicates Christ's atoning death; the forgiveness flows from the completed work of the one God raised. The forensic claim — God raised the one who bore sin's penalty — is the basis for the offer of forgiveness to everyone who believes.
39: 'And by him everyone who believes is freed from everything from which you could not be freed by the law of Moses' — the Christological superiority claim: the law of Moses could not free; Christ does. Dikaioō (justified, freed, acquitted) is the Pauline term for the forensic standing before God that the law could not provide (cf. Romans 3:20-26; Galatians 2:16). The 'everyone' (pas ho pisteuōn) is the universal-scope claim: not restricted to covenant members but available to all who believe. This is the Pauline gospel in its Acts form.
40: 'Beware, therefore, lest what is said in the Prophets should come about: 'Look, you scoffers, be astounded and perish; for I am doing a work in your days, a work that you will not believe, even if one tells it to you'' — the Habakkuk citation (Habakkuk 1:5) is a warning that divine acts exceed human expectation and cannot be dismissed. The Christological urgency: the resurrection is 'a work you will not believe, even if one tells it to you' — the category-breaking event that human expectation cannot contain. Dismissal of the resurrection is the fulfillment of the prophetic warning against scoffing at divine action.
41: The citation ends with Habakkuk's warning of perishing for those who refuse to believe God's astonishing work. The Christological offer is simultaneously a Christological warning: the same resurrection that offers forgiveness and justification to believers announces judgment for those who reject it. Christ crucified is 'a fragrance from life to life' for some and 'from death to death' for others (2 Corinthians 2:15-16) — the response to the Christological proclamation determines its effect.
42: As Paul and Barnabas left the synagogue, the people — both Jews and devout converts — asked them to speak again the next Sabbath. The Christological reception: the first preaching created desire for more. The gospel of the risen Christ is its own most effective apologetic — it raises questions it also answers, creates hunger it is designed to satisfy. The invitation to return is the Spirit's own work in the hearts of those who heard.
43: After the meeting broke up, many Jews and devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas, who urged them 'to continue in the grace of God' (prosmenein tē chariti tou theou). The pastoral follow-up is Christologically grounded: the grace in which new believers are urged to remain is the grace embodied in the crucified-and-risen Lord. Perseverance in grace is perseverance in Christ — not mere doctrinal assent but ongoing orientation toward the one in whom grace is personally encountered.
44: The following Sabbath, almost the whole city gathered to hear the word of the Lord — the scale of the response demonstrates the gospel's appeal across the entire social spectrum of Pisidian Antioch. The Christological word draws the city to itself. The 'word of the Lord' (ton logon tou theou) is the message about the risen Jesus; when proclaimed faithfully, it generates its own audience. The Spirit who raised Christ from the dead is the same Spirit who draws the cities of the world toward the proclamation of that resurrection.
45: When the Jews saw the crowds they were filled with jealousy and began contradicting what Paul said, blaspheming (blaspēmountes). The Christological conflict: the success of the proclamation provokes the opposition. This is the pattern that recurs in every city: initial openness, then synagogue resistance when the scale of Gentile response threatens the established community's identity. The blasphemy against the Christological proclamation is the most serious form of rejection in Acts.
46: 'It was necessary that the word of God be spoken first to you. Since you thrust it aside and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we are turning to the Gentiles' — the pivotal programmatic declaration. The 'first to you' reflects the Christological mission order (Romans 1:16 — 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek'); the rejection by the synagogue leadership becomes the mechanism for the Gentile expansion. The Christological mission strategy is not Plan B — the Gentile inclusion was always the plan (Isaiah 49:6, cited in Acts 13:47). The Jewish rejection does not defeat the plan; it triggers its next stage.
48: 'And when the Gentiles heard this, they began rejoicing and glorifying the word of the Lord, and as many as were appointed to eternal life believed' (hosoi ēsan tetagmenoi eis zōēn aiōnion episteusan) — the Christological election vocabulary arrives: 'appointed to eternal life' is the divine prior ordination that belief expresses rather than creates. The Christological proclamation reveals who has been appointed — those appointed believe when they hear. The joy and glorifying is the Spirit's own response in those whose appointment is activated by the Christological word.
49: The word of the Lord spread through the whole region — the pattern of exponential expansion from a single synagogue proclamation to an entire geographic region is the Christological mission's consistent dynamic. The seed of the word produces harvest far beyond what the initial sowing could predict. Christ's own parable of the sower (Luke 8:4-15) described this precisely: the scattered word lands in prepared soil and produces a hundredfold return.
50: The devout women of high standing and the leading men of the city stirred up persecution against Paul and Barnabas and drove them out of the district. The Christological irony: the most religious and socially prominent members of the city become the instruments of persecution. Social status and religious reputation do not guarantee reception of the Christological message; often they correlate with resistance. The cross is 'a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles' (1 Corinthians 1:23) precisely for those whose religious or intellectual identity is most invested in alternative frameworks.
51: Paul and Barnabas shook the dust from their feet — the Christological gesture of repudiation (Luke 9:5; Acts 10:11 — Jesus instructed his disciples to do this when rejected) — and went to Iconium. The gesture transfers moral responsibility to the rejecters; the missionaries have fulfilled their obligation. The Christological model: Jesus himself did not continue indefinitely in places of sustained rejection (Luke 13:34-35 — 'how often would I have gathered your children…'), and the pattern continues through his apostles.
52: 'And the disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit' — the closing note on the Pisidian Antioch community after the apostles' expulsion is Christological testimony. The community that remained after Paul and Barnabas were driven out is filled with joy and the Spirit — not despite the persecution but within it. The Christological community's joy in suffering is the signature of the resurrection faith: 'Count it all joy when you meet trials of various kinds' (James 1:2). The Spirit who raised Christ sustains the joy of those who suffer for Christ.
